# State and Memory

The project uses several layers of state, each with a different lifetime.

## App State

`state/AppStateStore.ts` defines the main application state shape. It includes UI state, task state, permission state, plugin state, notifications, model state, and session-specific fields.

`state/store.ts` is a small immutable store implementation with a getter, a setter, and subscription support. It is intentionally simple so React and non-React code can share the same source of truth.

## Session and Transcript State

The runtime keeps session-level state in several places:
- `bootstrap/state.ts` for app-wide bootstrap state
- transcript files for conversation history
- `utils/sessionStorage.ts` for session metadata and persistence helpers

This makes the agent recoverable across resume flows and background task transitions.

## Memory Folders

The repo also contains memory-oriented folders such as `memdir/` and `tasks/`-adjacent persistence paths. These are part of the system's durable working memory, not just temporary UI state.

## Why This Layer Exists

The agent needs more than a prompt and a response. It needs to remember:
- which tasks are running
- which tool calls were already made
- which permissions were granted or denied
- which files were touched
- which sessions can be resumed

That state is what makes the CLI feel continuous rather than stateless.
